title: Executive Order 12722—Blocking Iraqi Government Property and Prohibiting Transactions with Iraq

flags.justification: Broad blocking authority and wide implementation discretion for the Secretary of the Treasury, a standard structure for IEEPA-based sanctions orders.
No review process for affected persons is specified in this order's own text.
Multiple prohibited-transaction categories (import, export, transportation, credit, travel) all coherently serve one purpose: a comprehensive sanctions response to Iraq's invasion of Kuwait.

Sec. 2(h)'s catch-all anti-evasion provision ('any transaction... which evades or avoids, or has the purpose of evading or avoiding, any of the prohibitions') is broad and open-ended, in contrast to the more specifically enumerated categories elsewhere in the order.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
No review or reauthorization mechanism is specified despite being tied to a newly-declared national emergency.
The order is explicitly transmitted to Congress, a real transparency feature.
No external check is specified on the Secretary of the Treasury's exercise of blocking authority.

This is the United States' first, immediate legal response to Iraq's invasion of Kuwait, issued the same day as the invasion — establishing the initial sanctions framework for what became a major international response.
Sec. 4's redelegation and multi-agency cooperation provisions reflect coordination, not conflict.
The exclusions for publications and humanitarian donations (Sec. 2(b)) are narrow, standard, purpose-consistent exceptions in sanctions law.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 6/22 = 27.3%. Issued the same day as Iraq's invasion of Kuwait, August 2, 1990.
